steps.title: Stop a loop the moment something happens

narration.default: The loops you've built so far end on a fixed condition — guess the right number, count to n, walk every item. Real programs often need a different kind of stop: keep going until something specific happens, then jump out. Today's keyword is break — Python's instant exit from any loop.

Type the title and an empty list:

print("Type a word to add. Type stop to finish.")

words = []

The square brackets create an empty list — no items yet. The kid will type words one at a time and the list will grow.

steps.title: A loop with no end condition

narration.default:
Type:

while True:
    word = input("Word: ")

while True is Python's way of saying "loop forever" — True never becomes False, so the loop has no stopping condition. The only way out will be break. Inside the loop, ask the kid for a word.

Still inside the loop, type:

    if word == "stop":
        break

break is standard Python. The moment Python runs break, it pops out of the nearest loop instantly — even if the loop's condition is still True. Without break, while True would run forever and the kid could never finish.

Still inside the while, but OUTSIDE the if (same indent as the if), type:

    words.append(word)

When the word isn't stop, this line runs and the word joins the list. When the word is stop, break has already fired and Python never reaches this line.

Back to NO indent — outside the while — type:

print("Your words:", words)

This line only runs after break has fired. The indentation tells the whole story: indented lines are inside the loop, the un-indented line below is what happens after.
Click Run. Type apple, then banana, then mango, then stop. The terminal shows your three words gathered in a list. Run again with anything you like — the kid decides when the loop ends.

while True with break is one of Python's most useful loop patterns. Chat bots that listen until quit. Games that loop until game-over. Calculators that compute until the kid says done. Any time the program should keep going until SOMETHING happens — break is the way out.
